system_prompt:
You are extracting load-bearing passages from one chapter of a guitar method book.

The user will give you the verbatim text of one chapter. Extract every passage
that does any of the following:

- teaches a technique (how to play, fingering, hand position, picking pattern)
- defines a concept (a chord type, a scale, a position system, a terminology)
- gives a rule (when to do X, what to avoid, what to prefer)
- shows a load-bearing example (an exercise that illustrates a method's
  characteristic move)

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  { "quotes": [
      { "topic": "<short topic label>",
        "verbatim_quote": "<exact substring of the input>",
        "why_load_bearing": "<one sentence on why this passage matters>",
        "page": <int, optional>
      },
      ...
  ] }

CRITICAL RULES:
1. `verbatim_quote` MUST be an EXACT substring of the input text. Do not
   paraphrase. Do not add words. Do not fix typos. Copy character-for-character.
2. If you can't find a substantial passage in the input that fits a topic,
   skip the topic — don't fabricate.
3. Skip prose that is purely biographical, motivational, or transitional.
   Keep prose that teaches.
4. Aim for 3-15 quotes per chapter. A chapter with only one load-bearing
   passage gets one quote; a dense chapter gets many.
5. `topic` is a short label (2-6 words). Use the book's own terminology
   when possible.
6. `why_load_bearing` is one sentence in YOUR voice explaining the
   significance — not a paraphrase of the quote.

 The guitar fretboard is a kind of matrix that we must visualize very well. In order to master

     Any chord is built based on the notes of a given scale, and the vast majority of them are

Chord Symbols:                                                                                               4668




To represent the different types of musical chords, we use letters, acronyms, numbers and
musical symbols such as sharps and 7lats.
1) The letters represent the bass note of the chord, and also can indicate when a chord is
minor by using a small letter "m" right after the bass note letter.
Example: Cm (which means C minor)
2) The numbers represent the intervals contained in the chord. Actually we do not use
them for triads, only for chords with tension notes such tetrads or extended chords.
3) The acronyms represent diminished chords and augmented chords: Example: C dim
(which means C diminished) and C aug (which means C augmented). But below we have
more options on how to represent chords of this type.
Chord inversions are indicated by a slash plus the letter of the bass note, which will
indicate the inversion to be performed. We call these chords, slash chords.
Example: C major in the 7irst inversion is written: C/E, which reads C over E.

These simple chord shapes are essential to help us visualize chords, scales, licks, and
everything related to the guitar fretboard. But they can seem dif<icult to memorize at <irst

one using intervals going to the left side of the root, and another one using intervals going
to to the right side of the root. However these shapes might not work for some chords.

Note: When we say “right and left side”, we are referring to the perspective of the person

What about the 3rd Inversion?                                                                           4668




Attention:
There is no inversion to diminished or augmented chords, because they are symmetric.
Although we will learn later about symmetrical chords and scales, let’s clarify this idea:
Symmetric chords are those that repeat themselves in a predetermined intervallic cycle.
The diminished chord, for example, repeats itself each minor 3rd, and the augmented
chords repeat themselves every major 3rd.

It is very important to remember that there is no 3rd inversion for major or minor triads because
3rd inversion only happens with tetrads, because they have 4 notes. However, we will use major
and minor triads to better visualize the 3rd inversion shapes. But before learning them, let’s
understand some important things about 3rd inversion chords.

3rd inversion for Minor Tetrads:                                                                     4668




Notice that we can have some voicing concerns with major 7th and minor 7th (3rd inversion)
chords, because they will create an interval of a flat 9th between the bass note (the 7th) and the
root. So in order to solve this issue, we replace the root by the major 7th, thus repeating the
bass note one octave higher. At the end of this page we have an example of that.

Note: For major and minor chords, we should avoid intervals of a flat 9th between the bass note
and the top note of the chord.

     Chord Voicings                                                                               4668




     After learning the Tetrad Group Families, we must learn the many ways of distributing
     these notes, and that's what we call Chord Voicing. The choice of notes for a chord
     sequence, most of the time must keep the smallest possible movement between the notes
     from one chord to another. Certainly, sometimes there may be deliberate leaps, but the
     smallest movement will almost always be the best way. The Birst approach we will learn
     is called Drop Chords, and they are divided into two types:

     a) Drop 2: we drop the second highest note of the tetrad to the bottom note of the tetrad.
     b) Drop 3: we drop the third highest note of the tetrad to the bottom note of the tetrad.

     It is very important to understand that drop chord voicings can only be used with tetrads,
     leaving behind the tensions 9th, 11th and 13th that will be used later with the extended
     chords.

     Obviously, for each harmonic instrument we have a speciBic way of distributing the chord
     notes, specially when we talk about the piano, that we can play easily the extended chords
     by mastering two different chords (triads or tetrads). The following approach can be used
     for all instruments, and is a tremendous asset to improve your vocabulary of chords.

     The term “Suspended” or “sus” (an acronym used in popular music) comes from a
     counterpoint technique in classical music, and it means to sustain the 4th or 2nd of a
     certain chord, carrying these 2 notes to the next chord, thus delaying the resolution. But in
     modern popular harmony the notes of a chord do not need to be resolved as being part
     of a chord progression. Suspended chords are those where the 3rd is replaced by the 4th
     or 2nd. We call these chords "sus2" and "sus4”. Although the two instances exist, the most
     common is to replace the 3rd by the 4th. It is important to understand that by inverting
     the “sus4” chord we can Iind 3 more different “sus” chords. (EX. in te key of “C”).


                               www                ww
      &         www                                w
              1, 4, 5        4, 5, 1            5, 1, 4


     Although the three chords above are the same, (one chord in the root position and its two
     inversions), let’s consider each one of them as being in the root position. So we have:


               Csus4          Fsus2            G7sus4
                               www                 ww
      &         www                                 w
                                  68
     As we can see, the suspended chords have a connection with the dominant 7th chord

                         46
     family, because one of the inversions of it is a G7sus4, which actually can be written using
     the chord symbol: G7sus (there is no need to specify the symbol “sus4”. The use of “sus”
     will already indicate the suspension of the 4th. On the other hand, the “sus2” chord, must
     always be speciIied). In jazz, we usually add some extended notes such 9th and 13th, to the
     "sus" chord. In this case, we do not have to specify the 7th or the 4th in the chord symbol,
     we can simply use G9sus or G13sus, or Gsus9 and Gsus13 (both chord symbols work, but
     let’s consider the second option as the main one).

Extended chords are chords that contain extended notes beyond the 7th (9th, 11th, 13th).     4668




They are basically tertian chords, but there are several exceptions where the
superimposition of thirds between each note does not apply. So, let's consider all the
chords with extended notes as being extended chords regardless of the interval patterns
of the 3rds.
Attention:
a) Some chords may belong to more than one group of extended chords;
b) The extended notes 9, 11, 13 may appear in the chords as 2, 4, 6 depending on the
   chord voicing, but there is no diference.


Types of Extended Chords:
(in the key of “C” as example)

Note: Let’s consider the “sus” chords as part of the dominant chord family.


• 9th Chords
(Root, 3rd, 5th, 7thor 6th, 9th )

The 9th chord is a seventh chord with an added 9th. There are basically four types of 9th
chords with some variations. Below are some examples:


                              68
• Major 9th chord: Cmaj7(9), C6(9), C+maj7(9), Cmaj7(#9)
• Minor 9th chords: Cm7(9), Cm6(9), Cm(maj7)(9)

                     46
• Dominant 9th chords : C7(9), C7(b9), C7(#9), Csus9, Csus(b9)


• 11th Chords
(Root, 3rd, 5th, 7thor 6th, 9th , 11th )

The 11th chord is a 9th chord with an added 11th. However, the 9th does not have to be
part of the chord all the time. Examples of this are:
1-) When the chord might be impossible to play on the guitar;

2-) When we omit the 9th because of a chord voicing choice.

                          2019 © Jupiter Musical Group Ltd. All Rights Reserved             106


      There are basically 3 types of 11th chords with some variations. For major chords and
                                                                                                      4668




      dominant 7th chords, most of the time we use the #11th (#4) to prevent unwanted dissonance
      with the major 3rd. Only in some specific cases do we use the major 3rd and the perfect 11th
      (4th) together. However, for minor chords the perfect 11th (4th) can be used freely. See some
      examples below:

                th
      • Major 11 chords: not used often.
                th

                   th
      • Dominant 11 chords: C7(#11), C7(9)#11, Csus, Csus9, Csus(b9)



  • 13th Chords
      (Root, 3rd, 5th, 7thor 6th, 9th , 11th , 13th)

      The 13th chords are 11th chords with an added 13th. However, the 9th , 11th or both, do
      not have to be part of the chord all the time. Below are some examples:

      1- When the chord might be impossible to play on the guitar;
      2- When we omit one or more extended notes because of a chord voicing choice.

      There are basically three types of 13th chords with some variations. (Note: Notice that in

                                    68

It is important to understand that some extended chords can be “tricky”, belonging to
more than one group, or even seeming not to belong to any one group. That is the case of
two of these chord skeletons (in the key of “C” as examples): Csus9 and Cmaj7/6

The examples above can look easy. However, we will have some issues trying to name them.
As we have learned previously, we do not have inversions for extended chords, but the two
examples in question can be inverted even being part of extended chord groups.

In the case of Csus9 chord, we can classify it as being part of the 9th or 11th chord groups,
because it has the 9th but also has the 11th(4th). In the case of Cmaj7/6, we could classify
it as being part of the tetrad group or 13th chord group. So, as you can see there are many
holes in the musical rules. But music is not an exact science and that is the beauty of it.

Extended Chords on the Fretboard                                                                       4668




Next, we will have many types of extended chords built on the 6th and 5th strings. In addition
to each one of these two chords, we have 4th string chords derived from each one of them. It is
important to remember that the 4th string chords will be the 6th or 5th string chords starting
from the 3rd, 5th, 7th or even 6th.

Another important detail to pay attention to is that extended chords having the root on the 5th
string, most of time cannot be done exactly the same when moving them to the 6th string,
forcing us to leave or change some extended notes. However, when we do that, the extended
chord might change from it's original chord family. For example, if we add a 13th onto a 9th
chord, it will change its chord family from the 9th to the 13th chord family.



About Chord Families:
The chord families are just a better way to organize these chords, nothing else. We have to be
very flexible in order to understand that the families can overlap in many cases, sometimes
causing confusion as to which family they belong to. For example, we could consider the "sus"
chord as being part of the 9th chord family or the 11th chord family, but actually it belongs to its
own family, the "Suspended Chords". The same is true with many chords belonging to the
Added Tone Chord Family, which can be considered as being part of the 9th chords as well. So,
do not be afraid of what you name them, because the most important thing is to memorize and
visualize the chords the best you can.


About Omitted 3rd chords:
                               68
                      46
Chords with no 3rd (omitted 3rd chords) are built from major chords with a different bass
note.

For example: Cmaj7(omit 3rd), can be also called G major chord over “C” (G/C). This type of
chord sometimes may not work on the 4th string, because it will become ordinary sounding
without the root note. So depending on the situation, sometimes the best option is to play the
omitted 3rd chords on the 5th and 6th strings exactly the way they are (including the root).
